scenarios.name: Strait of Hormuz closure

cascade.channel: global supply shock - Hormuz carries ~20 Mb/d of irreplaceable world flow

spr.summary: HOLD - reroute covers demand; SPR stays whole as insurance (47,624 kbbl intact)

brief: SITUATION - Strait of Hormuz closure: 2,313 kb/d of import supply cut (Hormuz).
REROUTE - KARNADHAR: fully feasible reroute; every barrel grade-matched; naive plan infeasible (1 blend-limit breach(es)). +66 VLCC-equivalents tied up.
PROCUREMENT PRIORITY - secure marginal barrels first: Colombia (Vasconia, $4.2k/day per kb/d) [LP shadow prices].
RESERVES - HOLD - reroute covers demand; SPR stays whole as insurance (47,624 kbbl intact).
ECONOMY - global supply shock - Hormuz carries ~20 Mb/d of irreplaceable world flow. Brent $190 (sensitivity $150-244), pump ~Rs126/L, CAD 0.7% -> 6.1% GDP.
SPILLOVER - hardest-hit other imports: LNG (natural gas) 53%, Fertilisers (urea/DAP/MOP) 30%.
SEQUENCE (per ORF review) - re-source first, bridge with SPR, demand-manage last.

routes_ranked.why: The optimizer committed 2,087 kb/d here, closing 90% of the Strait of Hormuz closure gap — the single largest grade-feasible corridor that survives. It sails the Cape of Good Hope clear of the closure, and its 31° / 1.6% S / 2.8% asph slate runs at 10 of 10 refineries. Landed $78.6/bbl (+$5/bbl above the cheapest survivor — it ranks first on secured volume, not price). Supply slack — more available than the reroute needs; no procurement urgency.

Scarce: one more kb/d is worth $4.2k/day to the system (LP shadow price) — buy incremental volume here.

scenarios.name: Hormuz + OPEC+ squeeze

cascade.channel: global supply shock - Hormuz carries ~20 Mb/d of irreplaceable world flow

spr.summary: HOLD - reroute covers demand; SPR stays whole as insurance (47,624 kbbl intact)

brief: SITUATION - Hormuz + OPEC+ squeeze: 2,313 kb/d of import supply cut (Hormuz).
REROUTE - KARNADHAR: fully feasible reroute; every barrel grade-matched; naive plan feasible but grade-blind. +62 VLCC-equivalents tied up.
PROCUREMENT PRIORITY - secure marginal barrels first: Colombia (Vasconia, $10.1k/day per kb/d), Russia (Urals, $5.9k/day per kb/d) [LP shadow prices].
RESERVES - HOLD - reroute covers demand; SPR stays whole as insurance (47,624 kbbl intact).
ECONOMY - global supply shock - Hormuz carries ~20 Mb/d of irreplaceable world flow. Brent $190 (sensitivity $150-244), pump ~Rs126/L, CAD 0.7% -> 6.1% GDP.
SPILLOVER - hardest-hit other imports: LNG (natural gas) 53%, Fertilisers (urea/DAP/MOP) 30%.
SEQUENCE (per ORF review) - re-source first, bridge with SPR, demand-manage last.

scenarios.name: Russia fully sanctioned

cascade.channel: supplier redirect - global supply intact; India loses the Urals discount + a re-sourcing premium

spr.summary: HOLD - reroute covers demand; SPR stays whole as insurance (47,624 kbbl intact)

brief: SITUATION - Russia fully sanctioned: 1,590 kb/d of import supply cut (Russia).
REROUTE - KARNADHAR: fully feasible reroute; every barrel grade-matched; naive plan infeasible (220 kb/d un-runnable). Usable shortfall 220 -> 0 kb/d. +13 VLCC-equivalents tied up.
PROCUREMENT PRIORITY - secure marginal barrels first: Colombia (Vasconia, $4.1k/day per kb/d), Qatar (Qatar Marine, $4.1k/day per kb/d) [LP shadow prices].
RESERVES - HOLD - reroute covers demand; SPR stays whole as insurance (47,624 kbbl intact).

ECONOMY - supplier redirect - global supply intact; India loses the Urals discount + a re-sourcing premium. Brent ~$86 (redistribution, not a shortfall); India's real cost = lost Urals discount ~$3.5bn/yr + re-sourcing premium -> CAD 0.7% -> 0.8% GDP.
SPILLOVER - hardest-hit other imports: Fertilisers (urea/DAP/MOP) 20%, Coking coal 15%.
SEQUENCE (per ORF review) - re-source first, bridge with SPR, demand-manage last.

scenarios.name: Hormuz closure + Russia sanctioned

cascade.channel: global supply shock (Hormuz) + India's lost Russian discount

spr.summary: RATION - max sustainable draw 2,117 kb/d still leaves 8 kb/d for demand management (100% of SPR consumed)

brief: SITUATION - Hormuz closure + Russia sanctioned: 3,902 kb/d of import supply cut (Hormuz + Russia).
REROUTE - KARNADHAR: partial reroute; honest residual gap 2,125 kb/d; naive plan infeasible (53 kb/d un-runnable). Usable shortfall 2,178 -> 2,125 kb/d. +40 VLCC-equivalents tied up.
PROCUREMENT PRIORITY - secure marginal barrels first: Colombia (Vasconia, $43.6k/day per kb/d), Angola (Girassol, $38.1k/day per kb/d) [LP shadow prices].
RESERVES - RATION - max sustainable draw 2,117 kb/d still leaves 8 kb/d for demand management (100% of SPR consumed).
ECONOMY - global supply shock (Hormuz) + India's lost Russian discount. Brent $190 (sensitivity $150-244), pump ~Rs126/L, CAD 0.7% -> 6.2% GDP; +$3.5bn/yr lost Russian discount on top.
SPILLOVER - hardest-hit other imports: LNG (natural gas) 53%, Fertilisers (urea/DAP/MOP) 50%.
SEQUENCE (per ORF review) - re-source first, bridge with SPR, demand-manage last.

scenarios.name: Red Sea suspension (Bab-el-Mandeb + Suez)

cascade.channel: freight premium - crude reroutes via the Cape; global supply intact

spr.summary: HOLD - reroute covers demand; SPR stays whole as insurance (47,624 kbbl intact)

brief: SITUATION - Red Sea suspension (Bab-el-Mandeb + Suez): 54 kb/d of import supply cut (Bab-el-Mandeb + Suez).
REROUTE - KARNADHAR: fully feasible reroute; every barrel grade-matched; naive plan infeasible (19 kb/d un-runnable). Usable shortfall 19 -> 0 kb/d. +1 VLCC-equivalents tied up.
RESERVES - HOLD - reroute covers demand; SPR stays whole as insurance (47,624 kbbl intact).
ECONOMY - freight premium - crude reroutes via the Cape; global supply intact. Brent $82 (sensitivity $82-82), pump ~Rs100/L, CAD 0.7% -> 0.7% GDP.
SPILLOVER - hardest-hit other imports: Edible oils 18%.
SEQUENCE (per ORF review) - re-source first, bridge with SPR, demand-manage last.

routes_ranked.why: The optimizer committed 24 kb/d here, closing 44% of the Red Sea suspension (Bab-el-Mandeb + Suez) gap — the single largest grade-feasible corridor that survives. It transits Hormuz (open in this scenario, but chokepoint-exposed), and its 31° / 2.55% S / 3.4% asph slate runs at 4 of 10 refineries. Landed $68.9/bbl (+$69/bbl above the cheapest survivor — it ranks first on secured volume, not price). Supply slack — more available than the reroute needs; no procurement urgency.
